title: Jurassic Park
author: Michael Crichton
essentialQuestion: Should scientific progress ever be limited?
branchRule: Parent selects either Argument or Discovery. The selected branch appears after every Notebook; the unused branch remains hidden from the student.
reflection: Take a moment to return to the Essential Question one last time.
Should scientific progress ever be limited?
Write one final reflection explaining how your thinking has grown from the beginning of the novel to the end.
Which character taught you the greatest lesson?
Which decision had the greatest consequence?
What responsibility do scientists have to society?
What responsibility do readers have after learning these lessons?
If you could summarize Jurassic Park in one sentence, what would you say?
The greatest stories are not the ones that simply entertain us.
They are the ones that continue shaping the way we think long after we close the book.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do ordinary events become extraordinary mysteries?
introduction: The opening chapters of Jurassic Park don't answer many questions. Instead, Michael Crichton carefully places clues throughout the story, inviting you to investigate alongside the characters. As you read, pay attention to details that seem strange, out of place, or unexplained. Those details will become important later.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe what happened to the worker who was rushed to the clinic in Costa Rica. What made his injuries difficult for the doctors to explain?
Retell the attack on Tina at the beach. What clues suggested that the creature responsible was unlike any animal the adults expected?
Summarize what you have learned about InGen so far. What information does the author reveal, and what does he intentionally leave unanswered?
Describe Dr. Alan Grant and Dr. Ellie Sattler's work before they receive John Hammond's invitation. Why do you think Hammond wants these particular scientists to visit his island?
List three important mysteries introduced in these chapters. Explain why each one makes you want to keep reading.

think.instructions: Answer thoughtfully and support your ideas with evidence from the reading.
questions: The reader knows something unusual is happening long before most of the characters do. How does this create suspense? Why is it more effective than revealing everything at once?
Scientists rely on evidence to reach conclusions. Based only on Chapters 1–5, what do you think is happening on Isla Nublar? Explain which clues led you to your conclusion.

connect: History is filled with discoveries that seemed impossible when they were first announced.
Think of a scientific discovery or invention that surprised the world. Why do you think people are often skeptical when they hear about something completely new?
quickWrite: Imagine you are an investigative journalist who has been assigned to report on the strange events occurring in Costa Rica.
Write one paragraph summarizing what is happening and explain why you believe the public should pay attention. Use only the evidence available in Chapters 1–5.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, keep a running list of every clue Michael Crichton gives you.
Ask yourself:
Which mysteries have been solved?
Which questions are still unanswered?
Which clue do you think will become the most important later in the story?

Argument.title: The Big Question
content: Every strong argument begins with a question worth debating.
Should scientific progress ever be limited?
You are a consultant hired after the Jurassic Park disaster.
By the end of this Pocket, you will present a recommendation to InGen's Board of Directors.
What evidence do you already have?
You're simply recording your starting position.

Discovery.title: Choosing a Research Question
content: Jurassic Park introduces dozens of scientific ideas.
Choose ONE topic that interests you.
Circle the one you find most interesting.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How does wonder affect the way people make decisions?
introduction: In these chapters, the mystery begins to unfold. Characters who once questioned whether the impossible was real now stand face-to-face with living dinosaurs. Pay attention to how each person reacts. Not everyone sees Jurassic Park the same way.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the journey to Isla Nublar. What clues suggest that the island is unlike any place the visitors have ever seen?
Retell the moment when Dr. Grant and Dr. Sattler first see the dinosaurs. Why is this moment so important to the story?
Explain how John Hammond describes Jurassic Park. What does he believe the park will accomplish?
Summarize Ian Malcolm's first reactions to the park. Why does he seem less impressed than the other visitors?
Describe the different reactions of Grant, Sattler, Hammond, and Malcolm. How does each character view Jurassic Park differently?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: John Hammond sees Jurassic Park as a dream becoming reality. Ian Malcolm sees it as a dangerous mistake. At this point in the story, whose point of view seems more convincing? Explain your reasoning.
Wonder can inspire discovery, but it can also cause people to overlook risks. Do you think the visitors are asking enough difficult questions about the park? Why or why not?

connect: Throughout history, new inventions have amazed people while also raising concerns.
Think about a modern technology that excites you. What benefits does it offer? What possible dangers should people consider before using it?
quickWrite: Imagine you are one of the visitors arriving at Jurassic Park for the first time.

Write a journal entry describing your first impressions of the island. What amazed you the most? What questions or concerns would you still have before agreeing that the park should open to the public?
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, pay attention to moments when characters ignore warnings or become overly confident.
Ask yourself:
Who is asking the hard questions?
Who seems blinded by excitement?
What warning signs are beginning to appear beneath the wonder?
The greatest dangers are often hidden beneath the most impressive achievements.

Argument.title: Building a Claim
content: A strong argument has one clear claim.
Jurassic Park should never have opened.
Jurassic Park could have succeeded with better planning.

Discovery.title: What Do You Already Know?
content: What do you WANT to know?

notebooks.focusQuestion: What happens when confidence replaces caution?
introduction: By now, the visitors have seen the wonders of Jurassic Park. The park appears organized, carefully designed, and completely under control. But as the tour continues, small cracks begin to appear. Pay close attention to the warning signs. Ask yourself whether the characters notice them... or simply choose to ignore them.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe how the dinosaur tour is designed to keep visitors safe. What systems are supposed to prevent accidents?
Explain Ian Malcolm's concerns about Jurassic Park. What does he believe the park's designers have failed to understand?
Summarize the problems or warning signs that begin to appear during the tour. Why do these problems concern some of the visitors?
Describe how John Hammond responds when concerns are raised. Why do you think he continues to believe everything will work out?
Explain how the different characters define "control." Do they all mean the same thing?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Ian Malcolm argues that complex systems eventually become unpredictable. Do you agree? Think about technology, businesses, schools, or even families. Can everything truly be controlled?
Which character seems to be making the wisest decisions so far? Defend your answer using examples from the novel.

connect: People often trust technology because it usually works.
Think about a time when a machine, computer, or system failed unexpectedly. What happened? What did that experience teach you about depending on technology?
quickWrite: Imagine you are one of the park's safety inspectors.

Write a report identifying at least three concerns you have noticed during your visit. Explain whether you believe Jurassic Park should continue operating or delay opening until those problems are solved.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, pay attention to every moment when someone says, "Everything is under control."
Ask yourself:
Is it really under control?
Which warning signs are growing more serious?
Which character is paying the closest attention to the evidence?
Sometimes the greatest danger isn't the problem itself. It's believing there isn't one.

Argument.title: Finding Evidence
content: Highlight three moments that support your claim.

Discovery.title: Finding Sources
content: Locate at least three reliable sources.

notebooks.focusQuestion: When everything begins to fall apart, what reveals a person's true character?
introduction: The visitors have spent much of the story admiring Jurassic Park's incredible achievements. In these chapters, the focus begins to shift. The park's carefully designed systems are tested, tensions rise, and decisions become more difficult. Watch how each character responds when the unexpected happens.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the new problems that begin to develop in Jurassic Park. Which warning signs become more difficult for the characters to ignore?
Explain how Ian Malcolm continues to challenge John Hammond's confidence. What evidence does Malcolm use to support his concerns?
Summarize the decisions made by the park's staff as they try to respond to the growing problems. Which decisions seem wise? Which seem questionable?
Describe how Dr. Grant responds to the events unfolding around him. What qualities make him different from some of the other adults?
Explain how the mood of the novel changes during these chapters. What details help create that change?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Many disasters happen because people believe they have more control than they actually do. Do you think overconfidence is becoming a greater danger than the dinosaurs themselves? Explain.
Good leaders remain calm when situations become difficult. Which character has demonstrated the strongest leadership so far? Support your answer with examples from the novel.

connect: Think about a time when a carefully made plan didn't go as expected.
How did the people involved respond? What made the situation better—or worse?
quickWrite: Imagine you are a member of Jurassic Park's safety team.

Write a memo to John Hammond explaining the three biggest risks you believe the park is currently facing. Recommend one action that should be taken immediately and explain why it should be the highest priority.
keepReading: As you continue reading, pay close attention to moments when characters must make difficult choices under pressure.
Ask yourself:
Who stays calm?
Who begins to panic?
Which earlier warning signs are now becoming real problems?
How are the characters changing because of what they are experiencing?
Great stories often reveal a person's character not during moments of success, but during moments of crisis.

Argument.title: Looking at the Other Side
content: Every good argument considers opposing views.
People who disagree with me would say...

notebooks.focusQuestion: When plans fail, what matters most: preparation, knowledge, or character?
introduction: By now, the park's problems are becoming impossible to ignore. The characters are forced to make difficult decisions with limited information and very little time. As you read, pay attention to how each person responds under pressure. The choices they make now will shape everything that follows.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the major challenges facing Jurassic Park during these chapters. Which problems have become the most urgent?
Explain how the park's employees respond to the growing crisis. Which characters take responsibility, and which seem unprepared for what is happening?
Summarize the decisions made by Dr. Grant during these chapters. What do his actions reveal about his priorities?
Describe how John Hammond reacts as events continue to spiral out of control. Has his perspective changed? Explain your answer using evidence from the reading.
Retell the most important event from these chapters. Why do you think Michael Crichton chose to include this moment in the story?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: A person's character is often revealed during a crisis rather than during times of success. Which character has shown the greatest courage or wisdom so far? Explain your reasoning.

Many of the problems in Jurassic Park began long before the first emergency. Looking back over the novel so far, which earlier decision do you believe has had the greatest impact on the current situation? Defend your answer.

connect: Think about a real-world disaster or emergency you have learned about in history or seen in the news.
How did preparation—or the lack of preparation—affect the outcome? What lessons can people learn before the next crisis occurs?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing a report for InGen after these events.

Identify what you believe is the single greatest mistake made by the park's leadership so far. Explain why you believe this mistake led to many of the problems now facing Jurassic Park, and suggest one change that could have prevented it.
keepReading: The story is entering its most dangerous stage.
As you continue reading, pay attention to these questions:
Which characters continue to grow?
Which characters refuse to change?
What lessons are the characters beginning to learn?
Which earlier choices can no longer be undone?
Sometimes the greatest challenge isn't surviving the crisis. It's facing the consequences of the decisions that created it.

notebooks.focusQuestion: Can people ever fully control nature?
introduction: The park was built on the belief that careful planning, advanced technology, and strict procedures could control the natural world. In these chapters, that confidence is tested more than ever before. As you read, pay attention to the difference between what the characters planned to happen and what actually happens.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the biggest problems the characters face during these chapters. How have the dangers changed since the beginning of the novel?
Explain how the dinosaurs behave in ways the park's designers did not expect. What assumptions proved to be incorrect?
Summarize the actions Dr. Grant takes to protect the children. What qualities make him an effective leader during the crisis?
Describe how the different groups of characters respond to the emergencies unfolding across the island. How do their priorities differ?
Retell the most suspenseful event from these chapters. Why do you think Michael Crichton chose to write the scene the way he did?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Jurassic Park was designed with backup systems, security measures, and detailed plans. Why do you think those safeguards still failed?
Do you believe the greatest danger on Isla Nublar is the dinosaurs themselves, or the decisions people made before the dinosaurs escaped? Defend your answer.

connect: People often create systems to make life safer and easier, but every system has weaknesses.
Think about a system you use every day, such as traffic lights, school rules, computers, or the internet. What could happen if that system suddenly stopped working? What backup plans should exist?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been hired as an emergency management consultant after these events.
Write one paragraph identifying the greatest weakness in Jurassic Park's design. Explain how you would redesign the park to reduce the risk of a similar disaster happening again.
keepReading: As the novel moves toward its conclusion, continue looking for patterns.
Ask yourself:
Which characters learn from their mistakes?
Which characters continue repeating the same mistakes?
How has your opinion of John Hammond changed since the beginning of the novel?
If you were making the final decisions, would you still believe Jurassic Park should exist?
Remember, the best stories don't just entertain us. They challenge us to reconsider what we believe.
Pocket Branches

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do fear and pressure influence the decisions people make?
introduction: The situation on Isla Nublar is becoming more dangerous with every passing hour. Characters are forced to make quick decisions, often with limited information and little time to think. As you read, notice how different people respond to fear. Some remain calm, others panic, and some become willing to take greater risks than ever before.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the biggest obstacles the characters face during these chapters. How have their goals changed since arriving at Jurassic Park?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to care for and protect the children. What decisions does he make that help keep them safe?
Summarize the actions taken by the park's staff to regain control of the island. Which efforts succeed, and which do not?
Describe one moment when a character must make an important decision under pressure. What options did the character have, and what choice was made?
Retell the most suspenseful scene from these chapters. What details does Michael Crichton include to build tension?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Fear can cause people to panic, but it can also sharpen their thinking. Which characters allow fear to control them, and which characters use fear to make wiser decisions?
If you had been one of the visitors trapped on Isla Nublar, what would have been your greatest concern at this point in the story? Explain why.

connect: People often have to make important decisions without having all the information they want.
Think about a time when you had to make a difficult decision quickly. What helped you decide what to do?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing a survival guide for future visitors to Jurassic Park.
Write one paragraph describing the three most important rules every visitor should follow if the park ever reopened. Explain why each rule is necessary.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, watch how the characters change because of what they have experienced.
Ask yourself:
Who has become a stronger leader?
Who continues making poor decisions?
What lessons are the survivors beginning to learn?
Which problems were caused by nature, and which were caused by people?
The greatest test of character is often revealed when there are no easy choices left.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What separates a survivor from someone who simply survives by chance?
introduction: By now, the danger is constant. Every decision carries consequences, and every mistake can be costly. As you read, pay attention to the choices characters make under pressure. Consider which actions are based on preparation, which are based on instinct, and which are driven by fear.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the greatest challenges the characters face during these chapters. How has the situation changed since the beginning of the novel?
Summarize the decisions Dr. Grant makes as he continues protecting Lex and Tim. How do his experiences help him lead?
Explain how the dinosaurs continue to surprise the characters. What behaviors or events remind everyone that these are wild animals rather than attractions in a theme park?
Describe one important decision made by another major character during these chapters. What were the risks, and what happened because of that decision?
Retell the most exciting or suspenseful event from these chapters. Why do you think it stands out as an important moment in the novel?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Some characters remain focused even when they are afraid, while others allow fear to control their decisions. What qualities help people make good decisions during a crisis?

Many problems in Jurassic Park could have been prevented before the park ever opened. At this point in the novel, who do you believe bears the greatest responsibility for what has happened? Explain your reasoning.

connect: Leaders often have to make difficult decisions that affect the safety of others.
Think about someone you consider to be a good leader. What qualities allow that person to remain calm and make wise decisions during stressful situations?
quickWrite: Imagine InGen has hired you to write a report titled "Lessons Learned from Jurassic Park."
Write one paragraph explaining the most important lesson the company should learn from the events so far. Support your answer with examples from the novel.
keepReading: The novel is moving toward its conclusion, but many important questions remain.
As you continue reading, ask yourself:
Which characters have changed the most?
Which mistakes continue to have lasting consequences?
What has the novel taught you about leadership, responsibility, and preparation?
If the crisis ended today, what lessons should the survivors carry with them?
Great stories often leave readers with questions that continue long after the final page.

notebooks.focusQuestion: When everything goes wrong, what can we learn from our mistakes?
introduction: The story is approaching its conclusion. Many of the characters are beginning to understand that the greatest dangers were never just the dinosaurs. As you read these chapters, pay attention to how the characters respond to failure. Some accept responsibility, while others continue to blame circumstances or hold onto false hope.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the most important events that take place during these chapters. How do they move the story toward its conclusion?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate leadership. What decisions does he make that protect those around him?
Summarize how the survivors work together. Which characters depend on one another, and how does teamwork help them overcome new challenges?
Describe how John Hammond's perspective changes during these chapters. Does he fully understand what caused the disaster? Support your answer with evidence from the text.
Retell one event that surprised you. Why do you think Michael Crichton included this moment in the novel?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Many disasters happen because people ignore warning signs instead of learning from them. Which warning do you think should have been taken seriously from the very beginning? Explain why.
By this point in the novel, what do you believe is Michael Crichton's main message about science and human responsibility? Defend your answer using examples from the story.

connect: History is filled with inventions that changed the world. Some brought incredible benefits, while others created unexpected problems.
Think of a modern invention or technology. What responsibilities do inventors and scientists have after their invention is released to the public?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to speak before a committee investigating the Jurassic Park disaster.
Write one paragraph explaining what you believe was the single greatest cause of the park's failure. Support your opinion with evidence from the novel.
keepReading: Only a few chapters remain.
As you finish the novel, continue asking yourself:
Which characters have earned your respect? Why?
Which mistakes could have been prevented?
What lessons should future scientists remember?
Has your answer to the Essential Question changed since you began the book?
Sometimes the greatest value of failure is the wisdom it leaves behind.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What responsibilities come with knowledge and power?
introduction: You have reached the final section of Jurassic Park. The survivors are beginning to understand everything that has happened, but the consequences of the park's creation will continue long after they leave the island. As you finish the novel, think beyond the dinosaurs. Consider what Michael Crichton wants readers to understand about science, innovation, and human responsibility.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events that bring the story to its conclusion. How are the survivors finally able to escape the island?
Describe how the main characters have changed from the beginning of the novel to the end. Choose two characters and explain how their experiences shaped them.
Explain what ultimately happened to Jurassic Park. Why do you think the story had to end this way?
Summarize the lessons the surviving characters seem to have learned. Which lessons do you believe are the most important?
Looking back at the entire novel, describe one event that you believe changed the course of the story more than any other. Explain your choice.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the novel.
questions: At the beginning of the novel, the question seemed to be, "Can scientists clone dinosaurs?" By the end, the question has become much larger. What do you believe Michael Crichton ultimately wanted readers to consider about science and human ambition?
Return to the Essential Question: Should scientific progress ever be limited? Has your answer changed since you began reading Jurassic Park? Explain your thinking using evidence from the novel.

connect: The technologies discussed in Jurassic Park are no longer just science fiction. Today, scientists are working with artificial intelligence, gene editing, cloning, and other powerful technologies.
Choose one modern technology. Explain one benefit it could bring to society and one responsibility that should guide how it is used.
quickWrite: Imagine Michael Crichton asked you to write one final page for the novel.
Without changing the ending, write a paragraph explaining the lesson you hope every reader takes away after finishing Jurassic Park.
keepReading: Before moving to your Writing Booklet, take a few minutes to reflect on your journey through the novel.
Which character did you understand the most?
Which scene was the most memorable? Why?
Which discovery surprised you the most?
What question are you still thinking about?
If you could ask Michael Crichton one question about the novel, what would it be?
You are now ready to begin your chosen Writing Path.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do people respond when their plans no longer work?
introduction: By this point in the novel, the carefully designed systems of Jurassic Park have failed. The characters can no longer rely on technology or carefully prepared plans. Instead, they must depend on their judgment, courage, and ability to adapt. As you read, pay attention to who is willing to change and who continues to believe old solutions will still work.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Describe the greatest challenges the characters face during these chapters. How have their priorities changed since the beginning of the novel?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to lead and protect those around him. What decisions demonstrate his growth throughout the story?
Summarize the efforts made to regain control of Jurassic Park. Which attempts are successful, and which continue to fail?
Describe one important decision made by another major character during these chapters. Why was that decision significant?
Retell the most important event from Chapters 51–55. Explain why you believe it is a turning point in the novel.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: When people's original plans fail, they have two choices: adapt or refuse to change. Which characters have adapted the best throughout the novel? Which characters struggle to change? Explain your reasoning.
Do you believe Jurassic Park failed because the dinosaurs escaped, or because people misunderstood what they were creating? Defend your answer with examples from the story.

connect: Life rarely goes exactly as planned.
Think about a time when a plan had to change unexpectedly. What helped you adjust? What did you learn from that experience?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing a recommendation to a company planning another ambitious scientific project.
Write one paragraph explaining the most important lesson they should learn from the events of Jurassic Park. Support your advice with examples from the novel.
keepReading: As the story moves toward its conclusion, continue watching for the lessons hidden within the action.
Ask yourself:
Which characters have changed the most since arriving on Isla Nublar?
Which mistakes continue to create new problems?
What does the novel suggest about responsibility and leadership?
If you had been part of the original planning team, what would you have done differently?
Sometimes success isn't measured by avoiding every mistake. It's measured by what we learn after those mistakes are made.

Argument.title: Final Artifact
content: You have been hired as an independent consultant following the Jurassic Park disaster.
Write a professional recommendation to InGen's Board of Directors answering the question:
Support your recommendation with evidence from the novel and explain what the company should learn before attempting another project of this scale.

Discovery.title: Final Artifact
content: Write an informative research paper answering your chosen research question.
Explain not only what you learned, but why your topic matters today.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What is the difference between solving a problem and preventing one?
introduction: As the story continues, the characters are no longer simply trying to survive. They are beginning to understand why the disaster happened in the first place. As you read these chapters, pay attention to the difference between reacting to problems and preventing them before they occur.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events that take place during Chapters 56–60. How do these events move the story closer to its conclusion?
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant. Why was this decision necessary?
Explain how the characters continue working together. Which strengths does each person contribute to the group?
Describe one challenge the survivors face during these chapters. How do they overcome it?
Retell the event you believe had the greatest impact on the story. Explain why you chose it.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Many of the characters spend the novel solving problems that could have been prevented much earlier. Why do you think people often wait until a crisis before making important changes?
If you had been one of Jurassic Park's original planners, what single decision would you have changed before the park opened? Explain why you believe it would have made the greatest difference.

connect: Think about a problem in your school, community, or daily life that people usually react to instead of preventing.
What could be done ahead of time to reduce or eliminate that problem?
quickWrite: Imagine InGen is planning a second park.
Write one paragraph describing three rules the company must follow before reopening. Explain why each rule is essential.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, look for answers to these questions:
Which lessons have the characters learned?
Which lessons have they still failed to learn?
How have your opinions about the park changed since Chapter 1?
What responsibility comes with creating something powerful?
The final chapters are not just about escaping the island. They are about understanding the choices that led everyone there in the first place.

Argument.title: Evaluating Your Evidence
content: Before finalizing your recommendation, strengthen your argument.
For every reason you gave, ask:
Have I explained why this evidence supports my claim?
Would someone who disagrees understand my reasoning?
Add one new piece of evidence from the novel that makes your argument stronger.
Write one sentence explaining why this new evidence is especially convincing.

Discovery.title: Analyzing Your Findings
content: Good researchers don't simply collect facts. They explain what those facts mean.
Identify the three most important discoveries you have made.
How does it connect to Jurassic Park?
Write one paragraph analyzing the most important idea you have learned so far. Focus on explaining its significance rather than simply reporting information.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What does true leadership look like when there are no easy choices?
introduction: The characters are now making decisions that affect the survival of everyone around them. Some people continue thinking about protecting themselves, while others place the needs of others first. As you read, pay attention to the qualities that separate true leaders from people who simply hold positions of authority.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 61–65. Which event do you believe had the greatest impact on the story?
Describe how Dr. Grant demonstrates leadership during these chapters. What specific actions show that others trust him?
Explain one difficult decision another character must make. What options were available, and what choice was made?
Describe one challenge that the survivors overcome together. How did teamwork contribute to their success?
Retell one scene that increased the tension of the novel. What techniques did Michael Crichton use to make the scene suspenseful?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: A title does not automatically make someone a leader. Which character has earned the most respect through his or her actions? Explain your answer.

Good leaders often make difficult decisions that not everyone agrees with. Should leaders always choose the safest option, or are there times when taking a risk is the better choice? Defend your answer using examples from the novel.

connect: Think of someone you consider to be a strong leader.
What qualities make that person trustworthy? Which of those qualities have you seen demonstrated by characters in Jurassic Park?
quickWrite: Imagine a newspaper is publishing an article about the survivors of Jurassic Park.
Write one paragraph explaining which character deserves to be called the true leader of the expedition and why.
keepReading: As the novel continues, think about these questions:
Who has become stronger because of the crisis?
Who continues to make the same mistakes?
Which qualities are becoming more valuable than intelligence or technology?
How has your definition of leadership changed since the beginning of the novel?
Sometimes leadership is less about giving orders and more about making wise choices when others are depending on you.

Argument.title: Strengthening Your Counterargument
content: A strong argument acknowledges opposing viewpoints before responding to them.
Identify the strongest argument someone could make against your position.
Write one paragraph fairly explaining that viewpoint.
Using evidence from Jurassic Park, explain why your position remains stronger.
Your goal is not to defeat the other side with emotion. Your goal is to answer it with reason and evidence.

Discovery.title: Organizing Your Research Paper
content: By now you should have enough information to organize your paper.
Create an outline using the following structure:
Under each heading, list the evidence and sources you plan to include.
Identify any sections that still need additional research before you begin writing your final draft.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How should people respond after discovering they were wrong?
introduction: By this point in the novel, many of the characters are realizing that the world is far more unpredictable than they believed. Some admit their mistakes and change course. Others continue to defend decisions that have clearly failed. As you read, pay attention to how different characters respond when faced with the consequences of their choices.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 66–70. How do these events increase the urgency of the story?
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant during these chapters. Why was this decision important?
Explain how the dinosaurs continue to behave in unexpected ways. What assumptions about the animals have proven to be incorrect?
Describe how one other major character responds to the growing crisis. What does this reveal about his or her character?
Retell the moment you believe changed the direction of the story the most in these chapters. Explain why it was such an important turning point.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Admitting a mistake can be difficult, especially when many people are depending on you. Which character has done the best job of recognizing mistakes and adapting? Which character continues to resist change? Explain your reasoning.

Throughout the novel, technology repeatedly fails while human judgment becomes increasingly important. What do you think Michael Crichton is suggesting about the relationship between technology and wisdom?

connect: Everyone makes mistakes.
Think about a time when you realized you were wrong about something. What helped you recognize your mistake, and what did you learn from the experience?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to write a letter to John Hammond after the events of these chapters.
In one well-developed paragraph, explain one decision you believe he should have made differently. Be respectful, but support your advice with evidence from the novel.
keepReading: The novel is moving toward its final resolution.
As you continue reading, watch for these ideas:
Which characters grow because they learn from failure?
Which characters continue believing they were right?
What lessons are becoming clearer with each chapter?
How has your answer to the Essential Question changed since you first opened the book?
Great leaders are not people who never make mistakes. They are people who are willing to learn from them.

Argument.title: Refining Your Argument
content: Strong writers revise more than grammar. They strengthen their thinking.
Read your draft from beginning to end.
Does this paragraph clearly support my claim?
Have I explained my evidence instead of simply mentioning it?
Could a reader follow my reasoning?
Ask someone else to read your draft.
Write down one suggestion they gave you and explain how you improved your paper because of it.

Discovery.title: Writing Your First Draft
content: You have gathered your research, organized your ideas, and created an outline.
Now it's time to begin writing.
Introduce your topic, explain why it matters, and clearly state your research question.
Write your first body section using your outline and research notes.
Explain ideas in your own words.
Don't worry about making it perfect. The goal today is to get your ideas onto the page. Revision comes later.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What happens when survival depends on trust?
introduction: The crisis on Isla Nublar continues to grow. The survivors must rely on one another, often placing their lives in someone else's hands. As you read these chapters, notice how trust is built, tested, and sometimes broken. Ask yourself whether trust must be earned or whether there are moments when people have no choice but to trust others.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 71–75. Which event had the greatest impact on the story? Explain your choice.
Describe one situation where a character depended on someone else for help. Why was that trust necessary?
Explain how the dangers facing the survivors continue to change. What new challenges appear during these chapters?
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant. What factors do you think influenced his choice?
Retell the moment you found most suspenseful. How did Michael Crichton build tension during that scene?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Trust can be built through words or through actions. Which characters have earned the trust of others through their actions? Provide examples from the novel.
Do you think surviving a crisis depends more on intelligence, preparation, teamwork, or courage? Choose one and defend your answer using evidence from the story.

connect: Think about a time when you had to depend on someone else to accomplish something important.
What made that person trustworthy? What qualities helped you work together successfully?
quickWrite: Imagine you are interviewing one of the survivors after the events of these chapters.
Write one paragraph explaining what lesson that person would share about teamwork and trust. Use examples from the novel to support your response.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, pay attention to how relationships between the characters continue to develop.
Ask yourself:
Who has become the most dependable person in the group?
Which characters are learning to trust others?
Which characters continue relying only on themselves?
How does teamwork change the outcome of the story?
The greatest challenges are rarely overcome alone.

Argument.title: Strengthening Your Introduction
content: Your introduction is the reader's first impression.
Does it capture the reader's attention?
Does it clearly introduce the issue?
Is my claim easy to identify?
Revise your opening by adding one of the following:
a surprising fact from the novel
a brief description of the disaster
a powerful quotation from the text (if permitted by your curriculum)
Write one sentence explaining why your introduction encourages readers to continue reading your paper.

Discovery.title: Expanding Your Research
content: Strong research answers questions before readers ask them.
Highlight any places where a reader might ask:
"Is there evidence to support this idea?"
Use one additional reliable source to strengthen your paper.
Revise one section of your paper by incorporating the new information in your own words while properly citing the source.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What is the difference between confidence and overconfidence?
introduction: Many of the characters in Jurassic Park are experts in their fields. They are intelligent, experienced, and capable. Yet the park continues to fall apart. As you read these chapters, think about the difference between being confident in your abilities and believing you cannot fail.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 76–80. Which event most changes the direction of the story?
Describe one obstacle the survivors face during these chapters. How do they respond to the challenge?
Explain how the dinosaurs continue to behave in unexpected ways. What assumptions made by the park's designers have now been proven wrong?
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant or another major character. Why was that decision significant?
Retell one moment that increased the suspense of the novel. How did Michael Crichton keep readers wondering what would happen next?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Several characters believed they had planned for every possible problem. Why is overconfidence often more dangerous than a lack of knowledge?
At this point in the novel, do you believe Jurassic Park failed because of human error, flawed technology, or the unpredictable nature of life itself? Defend your answer.

connect: Think about a time when someone believed they were completely prepared for something but still faced unexpected challenges.
What lesson did they learn? How might that lesson apply to the events of Jurassic Park?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to speak to a group of young scientists preparing to begin their careers.
Write one paragraph explaining why humility is just as important as knowledge. Use examples from Jurassic Park to support your ideas.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, pay attention to moments when confidence changes into caution.
Ask yourself:
Which characters have become humbler because of their experiences?
Which characters continue believing they are in control?
What warnings are still being ignored?
What lesson is Michael Crichton building toward?
Knowledge is powerful, but wisdom comes from recognizing its limits.

Argument.title: Strengthening Your Body Paragraphs
content: Your reader should never have to guess why your evidence matters.
Highlight every sentence that presents evidence.
After each piece of evidence, ask yourself:
Have I explained how this evidence supports my claim?
Have I connected it back to the Essential Question?
Choose one body paragraph and improve it by adding stronger explanation rather than more evidence.
Evidence tells the reader what happened.

Discovery.title: Explaining Complex Ideas Clearly
content: One of the most important skills of a researcher is making difficult ideas understandable.
Choose one scientific concept from your paper.
Rewrite your explanation as if you were teaching it to a middle school student.
Avoid unnecessary technical language while remaining scientifically accurate.
Ask someone to read your explanation.
If they understand it without asking questions, you've written clearly. If not, revise your explanation until it is easy to follow.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do our choices today shape the future?
introduction: Every important decision has consequences. Some consequences appear immediately, while others take time to unfold. As you read these chapters, pay close attention to the connection between earlier choices and the events happening now. Ask yourself whether this disaster could have been avoided—or if it became inevitable long before the park ever opened.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 81–85. Which event do you believe has the greatest impact on the story? Explain your answer.
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant or another major character. Why was that decision necessary?
Explain how the survivors continue adapting to the dangers around them. What skills or experiences help them overcome new challenges?
Describe one moment that reveals something new about a character's personality or values. What did you learn from that moment?
Retell one scene that increased the tension of the novel. How did Michael Crichton keep you engaged as a reader?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Looking back over the novel so far, which single decision do you believe had the greatest impact on everything that followed? Defend your answer.
Some consequences are impossible to reverse once they begin. Do you think the disaster at Jurassic Park reached a point where success was no longer possible? Explain your reasoning.

connect: Many decisions in life have long-term consequences that are difficult to see at first.
Think of a decision someone can make today that might affect their future years from now. Why is it often difficult to recognize those consequences in advance?
quickWrite: Imagine you are preparing a presentation titled "The Five Biggest Mistakes That Led to the Jurassic Park Disaster."
Write one well-developed paragraph explaining what you believe should be the first mistake on your list and why it deserves that position.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, continue watching for connections between earlier decisions and later consequences.
Ask yourself:
Which choices are still affecting the characters?
Which lessons are becoming impossible to ignore?
What does the novel suggest about responsibility?
How has your understanding of the Essential Question changed since Notebook 1?
Every major consequence begins with a choice.

Argument.title: Writing a Powerful Conclusion
content: A conclusion should leave readers thinking long after they finish your paper.
Has your thinking changed since you began reading the novel? If so, revise your claim to better reflect your final position.
Restate your position in a fresh way.
Leave readers with one final thought about scientific responsibility.
Does my final paragraph challenge the reader to think?
If not, revise it until it does.

Discovery.title: Connecting Research to the Real World
content: Research becomes meaningful when readers understand why it matters today.
Add a new section to your paper titled:
How is your topic being studied today?
Why is it important to modern science?
What ethical questions does it raise?
How does it connect to the events of Jurassic Park?
Conclude your new section by explaining what you believe future scientists should learn from both your research and Michael Crichton's novel.

notebooks.focusQuestion: Can knowledge alone solve every problem?
introduction: Throughout the novel, the characters have relied on science, technology, experience, and intelligence. Yet every new solution seems to create another challenge. As you read these chapters, think about what knowledge can accomplish and where its limits begin.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 86–90. Which event do you believe changes the story the most? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one obstacle the survivors face during these chapters. How do they work together to overcome it?
Explain how the dinosaurs continue behaving in ways the park's designers failed to predict. What assumptions continue to prove incorrect?
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant or another major character. What risks did that decision involve?
Retell the scene you found most suspenseful. Which details made the moment especially intense?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: The people who built Jurassic Park were among the smartest scientists in the world. Why do you think intelligence alone was not enough to prevent disaster?
At this point in the novel, what quality do you believe has become more valuable than scientific knowledge? Defend your answer with examples from the story.

connect: Think about a profession where people have great knowledge but also carry great responsibility, such as doctors, engineers, pilots, or teachers.
Why is good judgment just as important as expertise?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been invited to speak at a conference for young scientists.
Write one paragraph explaining why scientific knowledge must always be balanced with responsibility and good judgment.
keepReading: As you continue through the novel, pay attention to the difference between knowledge and wisdom.
Ask yourself:
Which characters have both knowledge and wisdom?
Which characters have knowledge without wisdom?
How has your opinion of science changed since the beginning of the novel?
What lesson do you think Michael Crichton wants every reader to remember?
Knowledge gives people the ability to do remarkable things. Wisdom helps them decide whether they should.

Argument.title: Polishing Your Evidence
content: Strong arguments are built on clear, convincing evidence.
For every example you include, ask yourself:
Is this one of the strongest examples from the novel?
Would a reader understand why it supports my claim?
Have I explained the significance instead of simply retelling the event?
Replace one weak example with a stronger piece of evidence from a different part of the novel.
Write one transition sentence that smoothly connects two of your body paragraphs.
Your paper should guide the reader from one idea to the next without feeling disconnected.

Discovery.title: Evaluating Your Sources
content: Not every source deserves equal trust.
Review every source you have used.
Why should readers trust this source?
Identify one source that strengthened your understanding the most.
Explain why it was especially helpful.
Identify one place in your paper where adding another source would make your explanation stronger.
Locate that source and add the new information to your draft using proper citation.
Good researchers don't simply collect information. They evaluate it carefully before deciding what to believe.

notebooks.focusQuestion: When everything is uncertain, what gives people hope to keep going?
introduction: The survivors have endured challenge after challenge, yet they continue moving forward. In these chapters, pay attention to the choices characters make when success is no longer guaranteed. Consider what motivates people to continue when the outcome is uncertain.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 91–95. Which event do you believe was the most important? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one obstacle the survivors face during these chapters. How do they respond to it?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate leadership. Which actions show that he is thinking beyond his own safety?
Describe one important decision made by another character. Why do you think that decision mattered?
Retell one scene that increased the suspense of the story. How did Michael Crichton build tension during that moment?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Hope can give people the courage to continue during difficult situations. Which character seems to have the strongest sense of hope in these chapters? What gives that person hope?
Throughout the novel, some characters focus on solving immediate problems while others think about the larger consequences. Which approach is more important during a crisis? Defend your answer.

connect: Many people face situations where they cannot control what happens next.
Think of someone from history or your own life who remained hopeful during a difficult time. What attitudes or actions helped that person keep moving forward?
quickWrite: Imagine you are one of the survivors writing in your journal at the end of these chapters.
Write one paragraph describing what worries you most and what gives you the determination to continue.
keepReading: As you continue reading, watch for moments when the characters choose courage over fear.
Ask yourself:
What keeps the survivors moving forward?
Which characters have become stronger through adversity?
What lessons are beginning to emerge from the disaster?
How are the events changing each person's understanding of responsibility?
Hope is not pretending everything is fine. Hope is choosing to move forward even when the future is uncertain.

Argument.title: Strengthening Your Organization
content: Strong arguments flow naturally from one idea to the next.
Read your paper from beginning to end.
Does each paragraph support my claim?
Do my ideas appear in the best order?
Does each paragraph lead naturally into the next?
Write a topic sentence for each body paragraph.
Your topic sentence should introduce the main idea of that paragraph before presenting evidence.
Use connecting words and phrases so your paper reads as one continuous argument rather than a list of separate ideas.

Discovery.title: Incorporating Evidence
content: Researchers support their ideas with evidence, but they do more than simply quote sources.
Highlight every place where you present evidence.
After each piece of evidence, ask yourself:
Have I explained what this evidence means?
Have I connected it to my research question?
Does it help the reader better understand my topic?
Revise one section of your paper by adding stronger explanation after your evidence.
Understanding why those facts matter is even more important.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What is the true cost of ignoring responsibility?
introduction: The events on Isla Nublar are forcing every survivor to confront the consequences of earlier decisions. As you read these chapters, pay attention to the difference between accidents and choices. Ask yourself whether the disaster was unavoidable or the result of many small decisions made over time.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 96–100. Which event had the greatest impact on the story? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one important challenge the survivors face. How do they respond to the situation?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to grow as a leader. What qualities make others willing to follow him?
Describe one important decision made by another major character. What were the consequences of that decision?
Retell one scene that surprised you. Why do you think Michael Crichton included this moment in the novel?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: At this point in the novel, who bears the greatest responsibility for everything that has happened? Defend your answer using specific examples from the story.
If you could go back to the beginning of the novel and change only one decision, which would you choose? Explain why you believe that decision would have changed the outcome the most.

connect: History includes many disasters that investigators later determined could have been prevented.
Think of an event from history or current events where poor planning or ignored warnings contributed to a crisis. What lessons can people learn from studying those events?
quickWrite: Imagine you are serving on the government committee investigating the Jurassic Park incident.
Write one paragraph recommending the single most important safety rule that should be required before any similar scientific project is ever approved.
keepReading: The novel is entering its final stages.
As you continue reading, think about these questions:
Which lessons are becoming impossible to ignore?
How have the characters changed since arriving on Isla Nublar?
What mistakes could future scientists avoid?
If Jurassic Park had succeeded, would the ethical questions have disappeared?
Sometimes the greatest lessons come not from success, but from failure.

Argument.title: Editing for Precision
content: A convincing argument uses clear, precise language.
Circle any sentence that sounds vague or repetitive.
Replace those statements with specific details from the novel.
Have I clearly explained my ideas?
Have I used precise language instead of general statements?
Read your paper aloud one final time.
Mark any sentence that sounds awkward or confusing and revise it until it flows naturally.
Strong writing is usually the result of careful revision, not the first draft.

Discovery.title: Preparing Your Final Research Paper
content: Your research paper should now be nearly complete.
Today's goal is to make it ready for publication.
Confirm that every section supports your research question.
Make sure all borrowed ideas are properly credited and that your Works Cited or References page is complete.
Read your paper as though you know nothing about the topic.
Have I supported every major claim with evidence?
Make revisions wherever your reader might become confused.
Excellent researchers write so clearly that their readers never feel lost.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What happens when people refuse to respect the limits of nature?
introduction: Throughout Jurassic Park, the characters have struggled to control something far more powerful than themselves. In these chapters, consider whether nature can ever truly be mastered or whether it will always remain unpredictable. As you read, pay attention to moments when the characters discover that their plans cannot overcome the natural world.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 101–105. Which event most changes the direction of the story? Explain your answer.
Describe one challenge the survivors face during these chapters. How do they respond to it?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate wisdom and leadership. Which decisions show that he has learned from everything that has happened?
Describe one surprising behavior displayed by the dinosaurs. What does this reveal about the limits of the park's original plans?
Retell one scene that kept you on the edge of your seat. Which details made the scene especially suspenseful?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Throughout the novel, the dinosaurs behave according to their own instincts rather than human expectations. What message do you think Michael Crichton is communicating about humanity's relationship with nature?
Do you believe Jurassic Park failed because people underestimated nature, overestimated themselves, or both? Explain your reasoning using examples from the novel.

connect: People often try to control nature through technology, engineering, or science.

Think of a modern example where humans attempt to manage the natural world, such as weather forecasting, dams, wildlife conservation, or genetic engineering. What benefits does this provide? What risks still remain?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been invited to address a group of future scientists.
Write one paragraph explaining why respecting the limits of nature is just as important as making new scientific discoveries.
keepReading: As you continue toward the conclusion, look for the larger message behind the action.
Ask yourself:
What has the novel taught about the balance between science and nature?
Which characters have gained wisdom through hardship?
Which ideas introduced in the opening chapters have now come full circle?
What lesson do you think readers should remember long after finishing the novel?
Sometimes the greatest discoveries come not from controlling nature, but from learning to respect it.

Argument.title: Strengthening Your Voice
content: A strong argument sounds confident, thoughtful, and professional.
Listen for places where your writing sounds uncertain or repetitive.
with confident statements supported by evidence.
Replace general words with more precise language.
"Jurassic Park's security systems failed because they relied too heavily on technology while ignoring the unpredictability of living ecosystems."
Revise one paragraph to improve its clarity, confidence, and precision.
Your goal is to sound like a professional consultant presenting recommendations to InGen's Board of Directors.

Discovery.title: Explaining Cause and Effect
content: Researchers do more than present facts. They explain relationships.
Find three places where one event, discovery, or scientific principle causes another.
Add one paragraph to your paper explaining how your research topic connects to the events of Jurassic Park through a clear chain of cause and effect.
Strong research helps readers understand not only what happened, but why it happened.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do people make wise decisions when every option has consequences?
introduction: The survivors are running out of easy choices. Every decision now carries risk, and every action has the potential to affect someone else. As you read these chapters, pay attention to how the characters weigh their options before acting. Consider what separates a wise decision from a reckless one.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 106–110. Which event had the greatest impact on the story? Explain why.
Describe one difficult decision a character must make during these chapters. What options were available, and which choice was made?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to lead under pressure. Which actions show that he is thinking beyond the immediate problem?
Describe one obstacle the survivors overcome together. How did cooperation contribute to their success?
Retell one moment that changed your opinion of a character. What happened, and why did it affect your thinking?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Making good decisions often requires balancing courage with caution. Which character demonstrates this balance most effectively? Explain your reasoning.
If you had been responsible for making one important decision during these chapters, would you have made the same choice as the character? Why or why not?

connect: Sometimes there is no perfect solution to a problem.
Think about a difficult decision someone might face at school, at work, or in the community. How should people decide between two imperfect options?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing a leadership handbook using examples from Jurassic Park.
Write one paragraph describing an important principle that leaders should follow when making difficult decisions. Support your ideas with examples from the novel.
keepReading: As you continue toward the conclusion, watch for moments when characters must choose between what is easy and what is right.
Ask yourself:
Which decisions required the greatest courage?
Which characters continue growing as leaders?
What mistakes could still have been avoided?
What lesson is the novel teaching about responsibility under pressure?
Wisdom is often revealed not by having perfect choices, but by making the best choice possible with the information available.

Argument.title: Evaluating Your Argument
content: Before submitting your recommendation, step back and evaluate it as if you were a member of InGen's Board of Directors.
Read your paper from your audience's perspective.
Have I clearly answered the Essential Question?
Have I provided enough evidence to support my recommendation?
Would my reasoning persuade someone who disagrees with me?
The strongest evidence in my paper is...
One area I could still improve is...
Revise one section of your paper based on your evaluation.
Strong writers become their own best editors before anyone else reads their work.

Discovery.title: Drawing Conclusions from Research
content: Research is more than collecting information. It is discovering meaning.
Identify three important conclusions you can confidently make based on the evidence.
How does it connect to Jurassic Park?
Write one new section for your paper titled:
In this section, explain what scientists, engineers, or society can learn from your research topic and why those lessons remain important today.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What does it mean to take responsibility for your actions?
introduction: Throughout Jurassic Park, every major event has been shaped by the choices people made long before the crisis began. In these chapters, pay close attention to the difference between accepting responsibility and placing blame on others. Consider how true responsibility involves both acknowledging mistakes and learning from them.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 111–115. Which event do you believe is most important? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one challenge the survivors face during these chapters. How do they respond to it?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate responsibility and leadership. Which actions show that he places others before himself?
Describe one moment when a character faces the consequences of an earlier decision. How does that decision continue to affect the story?
Retell one scene that changed your understanding of a character. What new insight did you gain?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Some characters accept responsibility for their mistakes, while others continue making excuses. Why is accepting responsibility often more difficult than assigning blame?
Looking back over the novel, who do you believe has shown the greatest personal growth? What experiences contributed to that growth?

connect: Everyone makes mistakes, but our response to those mistakes often matters more than the mistakes themselves.
Think of someone you admire who admitted a mistake and worked to make things right. What made their response admirable?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing the final paragraph of a biography about one of the main characters.
Write one paragraph explaining what future readers should remember about that person's character, choices, and growth throughout the novel.
keepReading: As the story approaches its conclusion, continue asking yourself:
Which characters have truly changed?
Which lessons have been learned through experience?
What responsibilities come with knowledge and power?
What message do you think Michael Crichton hopes readers will remember?
Great stories remind us that while we cannot change our past decisions, we can choose how we respond to their consequences.

Argument.title: Final Peer Review
content: Every great writer benefits from another set of eyes.
Ask a classmate, parent, teacher, or friend to read your paper.
Which part of my paper is the strongest?
Which suggestion will improve my paper the most?
Which suggestion do I respectfully disagree with? Why?
Revise your paper based on the feedback you chose to accept.
Good writers listen carefully but make thoughtful decisions about their own work.

Discovery.title: Improving Clarity
content: Excellent researchers help readers understand difficult ideas with ease.
Read your paper from beginning to end.
Rewrite those sentences using simpler language without sacrificing accuracy.
Add one real-world example or analogy that helps readers better understand your research topic.
Would someone with no background knowledge understand my explanation after reading this section?
If the answer is yes, you've succeeded.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do people find the courage to keep moving forward after failure?
introduction: The survivors have experienced loss, fear, and disappointment, yet they continue searching for a way forward. As you read these chapters, think about what gives people the courage to continue after plans have failed and circumstances seem overwhelming.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 116–120. Which event most changes the direction of the story? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one challenge the survivors overcome during these chapters. What actions help them succeed?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate courage. Which decisions reveal his commitment to protecting others?
Describe one important interaction between two characters. What does this conversation or event reveal about their relationship?
Retell the moment you believe required the greatest courage. Why did you choose this scene?

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Courage does not always mean acting without fear. Based on these chapters, how would you define courage? Which character best demonstrates your definition?
Many characters have experienced failure throughout the novel. Which failure ultimately taught the most valuable lesson? Explain your reasoning.

connect: Everyone experiences setbacks.
Think about a time when someone failed but chose to try again. What helped them move forward instead of giving up?
quickWrite: Imagine you are preparing a speech for a group of future scientists titled "The Courage to Learn from Failure."
Write one paragraph explaining how the events of Jurassic Park demonstrate that failure can become a valuable teacher.
keepReading: The story is approaching its conclusion.
As you continue reading, watch for moments that answer these questions:
Which characters have grown the most?
Which lessons continue appearing throughout the novel?
What does Michael Crichton suggest about courage and perseverance?
How has your understanding of responsibility changed since Chapter 1?
Failure does not define a person. What they choose to do afterward often does.

Argument.title: Final Revision
content: Today's goal is to make every part of your recommendation stronger.
Review your paper using this checklist.
Does my introduction capture the reader's attention?
Does every body paragraph support my claim?
Have I addressed the opposing viewpoint fairly?
Does my conclusion leave the reader thinking?
Choose the section that still needs the most improvement.
Completely rewrite that section rather than making only small edits.
The strongest part of my recommendation is...
The greatest improvement I made during revision was...
Great writing is built through revision, not perfection on the first draft.

Discovery.title: Strengthening Your Conclusion
content: A strong research paper ends by helping readers understand why the topic matters.
Have I answered my research question?
Have I summarized my most important discoveries?
Have I explained why this topic matters today?
Add one final paragraph connecting your research to the future.
Where is this field of science heading?
What ethical responsibilities accompany future discoveries?
Finish your paper by writing one sentence you hope your readers will remember long after they finish reading.
Your conclusion should leave readers with understanding, curiosity, and something meaningful to consider.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What makes a legacy worth leaving behind?
introduction: The events on Isla Nublar are nearing their conclusion. The characters are beginning to realize that their choices will leave lasting consequences far beyond the island itself. As you read these chapters, consider what kind of legacy each character is creating through his or her actions.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 121–125. Which event do you believe has the greatest impact on the outcome of the story? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one important challenge the survivors face. How do they respond to it?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to change throughout the novel. Which actions in these chapters show how much he has grown?
Describe one moment that reveals an important lesson about leadership, responsibility, or teamwork.
Retell one scene that you believe readers will remember long after finishing the novel. Explain why it is so memorable.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: By this point in the novel, which character has left the most positive legacy? Which character has left the most harmful one? Defend your answers using examples from the story.
If the events of Jurassic Park were taught in a future science classroom, what lesson do you think students should learn first? Explain your reasoning.

connect: Every person leaves a legacy through the choices they make.
Think about someone whose actions have positively influenced your family, school, community, or the world. What choices helped create that legacy?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to write the final paragraph for an article titled:
"The Lessons of Jurassic Park."
Write one well-developed paragraph explaining the single most important lesson future generations should learn from the events on Isla Nublar.
keepReading: The novel is entering its final chapters.
As you continue reading, reflect on everything you have discovered.
Ask yourself:
Which lesson has appeared throughout the entire novel?
Which character has changed the most?
How has your answer to the Essential Question evolved?
What responsibility do scientists, leaders, and innovators share when creating something new?
The greatest legacy is not what we create. It is the wisdom we leave behind.

Argument.title: Preparing Your Final Recommendation
content: Today's goal is to prepare it for presentation to InGen's Board of Directors.
Review your paper one final time.
Does every paragraph support my recommendation?
Have I answered the Essential Question completely?
Is my evidence specific and convincing?
The three strongest reasons supporting it.
The most important lesson InGen should learn.
Imagine busy board members will read this summary before reading your full report.
Choose one sentence from your paper that best represents your argument.
If readers remembered only this sentence, would it accurately represent my position?

Discovery.title: Preparing Your Research for Publication
content: Scientists communicate their discoveries clearly and professionally.
Today's goal is to prepare your paper as if it will be published.
Write a brief Research Abstract (150–200 words).
Write a final statement answering this question:
How has researching this topic changed the way you understand Jurassic Park and modern science?
Your goal is not simply to present information, but to help others better understand both the science and the story.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How should people respond after discovering they have made a terrible mistake?
introduction: As the novel moves toward its conclusion, the characters can no longer change what has happened. They can only decide how they will respond. As you read these chapters, pay attention to the difference between regret and responsibility. Which characters merely regret the outcome, and which are willing to accept responsibility for their choices?

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 126–130. Which event most advances the story toward its conclusion? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one significant challenge the survivors face. How do they work together to overcome it?
Explain how Dr. Grant demonstrates both leadership and compassion during these chapters. What specific actions support your answer?
Describe one decision made by another major character that affects the outcome of the story. Why was this decision important?
Retell one scene that you believe best captures the central message of Jurassic Park. Explain why you chose that scene.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: By this point in the novel, which character has accepted the greatest responsibility for the events on Isla Nublar? Which character accepts the least? Defend your answer.
