Looking back over the novel so far, what do you believe has been the greatest cause of the disaster: human pride, poor planning, greed, technology, or something else? Support your opinion with evidence from throughout the story.

connect: Many inventions throughout history have brought both incredible benefits and unexpected dangers.
Think about a modern invention or technology that has changed society. What responsibilities do its creators have after releasing it into the world?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been invited to testify before a government committee investigating the Jurassic Park incident.
Write one paragraph explaining what you believe future scientists, engineers, and business leaders should learn from this disaster before beginning another project of similar size.
keepReading: Only a few notebooks remain.
As you continue reading, begin thinking about the novel as a whole.
Ask yourself:
Which lesson appears again and again throughout the story?
Which character changed the most?
Which decision shaped the entire novel?
If someone asked you what Jurassic Park is really about, how would you answer?
Great novels leave readers with ideas worth discussing long after the story ends.

Argument.title: Designing Your Final Artifact
content: Your recommendation is complete. Now prepare it for presentation.
Prepared for: InGen Board of Directors
Does it clearly communicate my recommendation?
Would a busy executive understand my position after reading only this page?
Choose one visual to include in your recommendation.
A timeline of the park's major failures
A chart showing the chain of decisions that led to the disaster
Your visual should strengthen your argument, not simply decorate your report.

Discovery.title: Creating Visual Explanations
content: Scientists often communicate ideas visually as well as through writing.
Choose one important concept from your research.
Write a caption explaining your visual.
How does it help readers understand your research?
The best visuals make complicated ideas easier to understand, not more complicated.

notebooks.focusQuestion: How do we know when something is too dangerous to continue?
introduction: Throughout Jurassic Park, the characters have tried to solve one problem after another. As you read these chapters, consider whether some projects should simply come to an end. Think about the difference between fixing a problem and recognizing that it can no longer be safely managed.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 131–135. Which event do you believe has the greatest impact on the novel? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one obstacle the survivors face during these chapters. How do they respond to it?
Explain how Dr. Grant continues to demonstrate leadership. What qualities make him someone others are willing to follow?
Describe one decision that changes the direction of the story. What were the consequences of that decision?
Retell one scene that demonstrates how much the situation has changed since the beginning of the novel.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Some ideas are simply too dangerous to continue, even if they are scientifically possible. At this point in the novel, do you believe Jurassic Park should be permanently abandoned? Explain your reasoning using evidence from the story.
Looking back over the novel, what warning sign should have convinced the characters to stop the project before disaster struck? Why do you believe that warning was the most important?

connect: History contains many projects that were stopped because they were considered too dangerous, too expensive, or too risky.
Think about a modern technology, invention, or project that has caused debate. Who should decide whether it continues, and what factors should they consider?
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing the final recommendation for a government safety commission.

Write one paragraph explaining the conditions that must exist before a large scientific project should be allowed to move forward. Support your recommendations with lessons learned from Jurassic Park.
keepReading: Only a few chapters remain.
As you finish the novel, continue asking yourself:
What lesson has Michael Crichton been building since Chapter 1?
Which characters have earned your respect?
Which mistakes should never be repeated?
If Jurassic Park had succeeded, would it still have been the right thing to build?
The most important question is not always, "Can we?" Sometimes it is, "Should we?"

Argument.title: Preparing for Your Presentation
content: A strong recommendation should be just as persuasive when spoken as when read.
Imagine you have five minutes to present your recommendation to InGen's Board of Directors.
Write a brief presentation outline that includes:
The most convincing piece of evidence from the novel
Your final recommendation to the board
Revise any sections that sound confusing, repetitive, or difficult to follow.
Write one sentence answering this question:
If the board remembers only one thing from my presentation, I want them to remember...

Discovery.title: Sharing Your Research
content: Research has little value if it cannot be clearly communicated.
Create a presentation outline for your research.
Explain how you will use it to help your audience understand your topic.
Write a closing statement that answers this question:
What is the single most important idea I want my audience to remember after learning about my research?
Your goal is not simply to share information, but to help others think differently because of what they have learned.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What should people do after a disaster to prevent history from repeating itself?
introduction: The survivors are nearing the end of their journey, but the story is about more than escaping Isla Nublar. It is about deciding what should happen next. As you read these chapters, think about how people should respond after a catastrophic failure. Is it enough to rebuild, or must they first understand what went wrong?

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 136–140. Which event brings the characters closest to resolving the conflict? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant during these chapters. How does this decision reflect everything he has learned throughout the novel?
Explain how the remaining characters work together to overcome the challenges they face. Which qualities contribute most to their success?
Describe one event that demonstrates the lasting consequences of the decisions made before Jurassic Park opened.
Retell one scene that you believe captures the central conflict of the novel. Explain why this scene is so significant.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: After a disaster, people often ask, "Who is responsible?" Why is it just as important to ask, "What needs to change?" Explain your thinking using examples from the novel.
Imagine Jurassic Park had survived the disaster and remained open. Do you think the ethical problems raised by the novel would have disappeared? Why or why not?

connect: Throughout history, accidents have led to important changes in laws, safety standards, and technology.
Think of a historical event where people changed the way something was done after a tragedy. What improvements were made, and why were they necessary?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to help write a new Code of Ethics for Scientific Innovation.
Write one paragraph describing the most important rule scientists should follow before introducing a powerful new technology to the world.
keepReading: Only two Notebooks remain.
As you finish the novel, begin thinking about the story as a whole.
Ask yourself:
What is the greatest lesson Michael Crichton wants readers to remember?
Which character experienced the greatest transformation?
Which decision had the greatest consequence?
Has your answer to the Essential Question changed since Notebook 1?
The purpose of history is not simply to remember the past. It is to build a wiser future.

Argument.title: Delivering a Persuasive Recommendation
content: Your report is complete. Now focus on communicating your ideas effectively.
Prepare a 2-minute summary of your recommendation.
What are my three strongest reasons?
Review your report one final time.
The sentence that best communicates your recommendation.
Write one paragraph reflecting on this question:
Has your opinion changed since Lesson 1?
If it has, explain what evidence changed your thinking.
If it has not, explain which ideas from the novel strengthened your original position.

Discovery.title: Preparing to Teach Others
content: One of the best ways to demonstrate understanding is by teaching someone else.
Create a one-page teaching guide for your topic.
Write three discussion questions that would help another student think more deeply about your research topic.
Avoid questions with simple right-or-wrong answers.
How has researching this topic changed the way you understand both science and the events of Jurassic Park?

notebooks.focusQuestion: What lessons are worth carrying into the future?
introduction: The story is almost over, but its ideas are just beginning. As the events of Jurassic Park draw to a close, think beyond the island. Consider what the characters have learned and what future generations should learn from their experiences. Great stories don't simply entertain us. They challenge the way we think about the world.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the major events from Chapters 141–145. Which event best prepares the novel for its conclusion? Explain your reasoning.
Describe one important decision made by Dr. Grant during these chapters. How does this decision demonstrate everything he has learned throughout the story?
Explain how one other major character has changed since the beginning of the novel. Use examples from both the opening and these chapters to support your answer.
Describe one conflict that is finally resolved during these chapters. How does its resolution affect the story?
Retell one scene that you believe captures the overall message of Jurassic Park. Explain why you chose it.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the reading.
questions: Looking back over the entire novel, what do you believe is Michael Crichton's most important warning to society? Defend your answer with evidence from throughout the story.
The characters entered Jurassic Park believing they understood nature. How has the novel challenged that belief? What new understanding have both the characters and the readers gained?

connect: Every generation inherits powerful new technologies.
What responsibility do you believe your generation has when developing or using technologies that previous generations could only imagine?
quickWrite: Imagine you have been asked to write the final recommendation in a report titled:
"Lessons for Future Innovators."
Write one well-developed paragraph explaining the most important lesson future scientists, engineers, and inventors should learn from the events of Jurassic Park.
keepReading: Only one Notebook remains.
As you prepare to finish the novel, begin reflecting on your entire journey.
Ask yourself:
Which character changed the most?
Which decision had the greatest consequence?
Which lesson surprised you the most?
If someone asked why Jurassic Park is still relevant today, what would you say?
The best books don't end on the final page. They continue through the conversations they inspire.

Argument.title: Final Reflection and Self-Assessment
content: Before submitting your recommendation, evaluate your work as a professional writer.
The strongest evidence in my report is...
The biggest improvement I made during this project was...
If I had one more day to revise, I would...
Review your report one final time using this checklist.
Write a closing statement to InGen's Board of Directors.
In two or three sentences, summarize your recommendation and explain why the board should trust your conclusions.
A professional recommendation is judged not only by its conclusions but by the quality of its reasoning.

Discovery.title: Final Reflection and Self-Assessment
content: Researchers continually evaluate both their work and their learning.
The most interesting discovery I made was...
The most reliable source I found was...
The biggest challenge I overcame was...
One question I still have is...
Review your research paper using this checklist.
Write a closing statement for your paper.
In two or three sentences, explain why your research matters and what you hope your readers will understand after reading your work.
The purpose of research is not simply to answer questions, but to inspire new ones.

notebooks.focusQuestion: What responsibilities come with the power to change the world?
introduction: You have reached the end of Jurassic Park. The dinosaurs, the excitement, and the danger were never the true point of the story. Michael Crichton used an unforgettable adventure to ask difficult questions about science, ambition, responsibility, and human nature. As you finish the novel, think not only about what happened on Isla Nublar, but about what should happen next whenever people possess the power to change the world.

narrate.instructions: Answer each question in complete sentences using your own words.
questions: Summarize the final events of the novel. How does Michael Crichton bring the story to a close?
Describe how Dr. Alan Grant has changed since the beginning of the novel. Which experiences had the greatest influence on his growth?
Explain what ultimately becomes of Jurassic Park. Why do you think Michael Crichton chose this ending instead of another?
Describe one lesson learned by a major character. How did that lesson develop throughout the novel?
Looking back over the entire story, identify the single most important event in Jurassic Park. Explain why you believe it changed everything that followed.

think.instructions: Support your answers with evidence from the entire novel.
questions: Return to the Essential Question. Should scientific progress ever be limited? Has your answer changed since you began reading the novel? Explain your thinking using evidence from throughout Jurassic Park.

Michael Crichton could have written a novel simply about dinosaurs escaping. Instead, he wrote a novel about people, choices, and consequences. What do you believe is the central message of Jurassic Park? Defend your answer with examples from the story.

connect: The world is entering an age of artificial intelligence, gene editing, robotics, and other rapidly advancing technologies.
Choose one emerging technology and explain how the lessons of Jurassic Park should influence the way society develops and uses it.
quickWrite: Imagine you are writing the final page of a textbook titled The Legacy of Jurassic Park.
In one well-developed paragraph, explain why students fifty years from now should still read this novel. What lessons make it timeless?
keepReading: Before moving to Reflection, spend a few minutes thinking about your entire journey through the novel.
Consider these questions:
Which character changed the most?
Which scene will stay with you the longest?
Which idea challenged your thinking the most?
What surprised you most about the novel?
What is one lesson from Jurassic Park that you can apply to your own life?
Great books don't simply tell memorable stories.
They help us ask better questions about ourselves and the world we are building.

Argument.title: Final Artifact
content: Congratulations! You have completed your recommendation.
Now prepare your final report for submission to InGen's Board of Directors.
After completing your report, answer the following questions.
What evidence from Jurassic Park most influenced your recommendation?
Did your opinion change as you read the novel? Why or why not?
What is the single most important lesson you hope the Board of Directors learns from your report?

Discovery.title: Final Artifact
content: Congratulations! You have completed your research project.
Prepare your final paper for publication.
After completing your paper, answer the following questions.
What was the most important discovery you made during your research?
How did your research change the way you understand Jurassic Park?
What future questions should scientists continue exploring about your topic?
